text: HARTING User’s Guide Transportation 1 General notes The information on components provided by this user’s guide should only be used for commercial purposes and applications. This infor- mation describes the components but must not be considered as a guarantee of certain properties. HARTING expressly reserves the right to make changes to components in terms of design or materials in response to quality improvements, advancements, manufacturing requirements or for other miscellaneous reasons, even without prior notice. Binding information will only be issued upon request by the appropriate HARTING employee. Country specific contact details and information in local languages can be found at www.HARTING.com. Further informa- tion on products and solutions can be found in the HARTING e-Shop at b2b.harting.com and in the catalogues “Industrial Connectors Han®”, “HARTING Device Connectivity”, “Circular Connectors” and “Connectors DIN 41 612”. It is the responsibility of the user to assess whether, in special areas of ap- plication not foreseeable by HARTING or not known to HARTING, regulations other than those already specified must be considered when using the com- ponents described in this document. Research and standards are constantly evolving and driving the current state of technology. HARTING has taken great care to ensure that this information is current and state-of-the-art. The user is nevertheless obligated to deter- mine the requirements for his application on his own responsibility and to comply with the relevant safety requirements. Beyond the mandatory legal framework, HARTING does not guarantee and is not liable for the correctness, completeness and timeliness of the speci- fications. In this respect and to this extent, HARTING is not liable for direct or indirect damages (including loss of revenue) resulting from or otherwise related to the use of the information found in this user’s guide. All rights are reserved by HARTING – including those of translation, copying, use of illustrations, remote transmission, photocopying or similar reproduc- tions, and/or computer archiving – whether in whole or in part. The repro- duction of patented names, trade names, trademarks, etc. does not entitle that those names, without special marks, as defined by trademark and trade protection legislation, can be regarded as freely usable by everyone. 2 General notes Standards and other technical regulations The standards, directives and other technical regulations cited in this docu- ment refer to the current editions available at the editorial deadline. Only the most current standard, however, is authoritative for the user of that standard. Featured links In this manual, individual keywords or product names are marked by yellow boxes. . . . 306 Table of contents 9 I I – Foreword I – Foreword The railway industry and the HARTING Technology Group have enjoyed a successful partnership marked by a long tradition of trust and cooperation. HARTING has delivered connectors and rail accessories to the railway industry for many years. The expertise and data that we have gathered over these many years of cooperation has provided the foundation for many of our innovations. This document is not a replacement for our catalogue; instead it provides an overview for the use of HARTING products and solutions in railway vehicles and stationary rail applications. We have brought together the information that we think will make the user’s job easier: whether that job is in construction, development or in production. Not only assembly instructions are included here – you will also find descriptions of the certifications, regulations and directives that are relevant to the railway industry (and with which HARTING products are naturally in compliance). This book is structured so that information relevant to specific groups can be found quickly and simply. Sample applications are used to provide the rea- der with an overview of the many uses for HARTING products. A selection of products based on connection methods, accompanied with the correspon- ding installation instructions, is also included. The products are organised and shown according to their applications, whether for indoor or outdoor usage, for data transfer or for carrying high currents. In other chapters, you can find information about the new range of HARTING current sensors and products for the ever-increasing area of network technology. Solutions such as Ethernet networks, for use on the station platform and on-board the train, are described here. HARTING also develops customised solutions jointly with the customer to meet their individual require- ments. You will find a selection of these solu- tions in Chapter VII. HARTING was founded as a company in 1945. Today, the HARTING Technology Group em- ploys over 400 engineers to design a variety of products and tailor-made solutions in the fields of machine construction, railway sys- tems, wind power facilities, factory automati- on and telecommunications. In May 2006, HARTING became one of the first companies in Europe to be certified for IRIS compliance (the International Railway Industry Standard). From this come the requirements and challenges that HARTING will continue to face in the future in terms of meeting the needs and wishes of the rail industry. 10 I I – Foreword Connections and network systems for railway vehicles Electrical connections for communication and power transfer are normally made using plug connectors. In recent years, such connectors have been playing a greater role in railway vehicles. This is because of the enormous time and cost pressures that ope- rators are put under. Modern railway vehicles have a modular design in order to help meet these requirements. As a result, vehicle components are often manufactured at different sites and supplied by different contractors. The individual components are then installed by the provider of the end system. Modularity offers the advantage of accelerating the initial commissioning process. It also significantly shortens the down time associated with maintenance and repair work. Connectors can play a very important role in a modular design. Since they can easily be plugged in and out, connectors make it possible for electrical systems to be swapped out quickly. The efficient plug & play capability allows you to save time during commissioning or re-commissioning. The complex process of wiring up the connection cables is a thing of the past. Within the past decade, HARTING has designed a product line more clearly aligned with the requirements of railway customers. Products used in railway vehicles must also be fully railway compliant because they are exposed to very extreme conditions including temperature fluctuations, moisture, vibrations, ballast strikes and electromagnetic radiation. What significance does all this have for the product? Robustness, reliability and durability: these are the key product requirements. A complete line of tests are carried out to ensure that HARTING products are good enough to meet these strict requirements. These tests are carried out by HARTING’s own certified laboratory and are also used during product development. These tests are subject not only to electrical standards (such as DIN EN 61984) but also to the appropriate railway standards (such as EN 50467, DIN EN 45545 etc.). A comprehensive product line, application expertise and excellent quality: these are the factors that have brought HARTING success in the railway sector. These are also the strengths that we will use to support our future collaboration. 11 II II – Application examples II – Application examples Table of contents II-1 Overview � � � � � � � � � � � � � � connectors into the design phase of railway vehicles All HARTING connectors and housings have been tested and approved for use in railway vehicles� A distinction must be made based on where the con- nector will be used on the vehicle� Hoods or housings with Han-Easy Lock® locking levers (Han® B and Han® EMC / B series) are suitable for indoor applications without restrictions� Among the outstanding features of the locking levers used within this series is the low force required to operate them� However, vehicle constructors may view the desired feature also as undesirable e�g� when plannig outdoor appli- cations� Under environmental impacts such as drifting snow, stone chipping due to swirling ballast stones or vandalism, there is the risk that the locking mechanism will open uncontrolled, which might lead to an interruption of the vehicle’s operation� In order to prevent any unintentional opening, housings are equipped with screw lockings for outdoor application (e�g� the Han® HPR series)� The screw locking technique has clear advantages over locking levers in such environ- ments� The operation of the connector can be better controlled� Along with the screw locking, Han® HPR hoods and housings are equip- ped with protection class IP68 (IP69K) in the mated position� They are protected against corrosion and have very good EMC properties� Providing the same level of corrosion protection – but using the smaller di- mensions of the Han® B series’ (and not equipped with screw lockings), the Han® M series offers a railway-compatible alternative for some applications� The robust stainless steel lever is designed so that the locking force is a bit higher than that of the Han-Easy Lock® lever� It is also a good idea to imple- ment additional measures, dependent on the installation location, in order to prevent an unintended opening� Such measures include blocking devices, protective panels and the proper choice of location for the installation� 14 II II – save time and costs during commissioning or servicing, electrical cabinets have a pluggable design, covering all media from power to signals and data� As an additional service, HARTING offers pre-assem- bled and tested connector sets as well as customised control cabinets� This means that the customer can benefit from sgnificantly shorter assembly times for electrical cabinets� II-11.1 becomes more efficient and flexible, if connectors are used instead of hardwired solutions� Mateable interfaces are not only space- saving compared to standard screw terminals – when crimp contacts are used, they are also maintenance-free� In addition, they enable the pre-as- sembly of separate units and protect against incorrect matings via coding� 22 II II – Application examples Such                                                 148 30 III III – Connectivity products III-1 Introduction This chapter introduces the products that are currently being used for elec- trical connections in the railway industry  Already at the earliest stages, the requirements of this industry feed into the design of the connectors  The following criteria should be considered when selecting a product: • The place of use and the environmental surroundings for the connector (a criterion for selecting the hood and/or housing) • Current and voltage (a criterion for selecting the contact inserts and con- tacts) • Wire construction (a criterion for selecting the type of termination) • EMC and shielding (a criterion for selecting the housing and cable clamp) The modular design of HARTING products allows almost all contact inserts to be combined with all types of hoods and housings  It is also possible to put together inappropriate combinations  Contact us if you are unsure Assembly principle of Han® HC Modular 41 III III – Connectivity products III-2.4.3 Han® HPR housing for rear sealing The Han® HPR series of hoods and housings for rear sealing are specially designed for applications that require an IP66 or IP68 / IP69K seal on both the device and the cable side  Such seals are typically required for panel feed- through connections that may be exposed to dust and moisture on both sides  The hoods and housings offer the rugged design, vibration resistance and sui- tability for harsh environmental conditions typical of the Han® HPR series  The connector system, which is closed on both sides, is particularly suitable for installations that connect two fire sections  The reason for this is the high degree of tightness, which prevents the spread of smoke gases into adjacent sections in accordance with EN 45545-3 (E15, E30)  The rear sealing can be realised both via so-called panel feed-through housings and via hoods  Cable entries from M32 to M50 are available for both variants, but smaller or multiple cable entries can also be provided  All the contact inserts can be used that fit in the corresponding Han® HPR housings  III-2.4.3.1 Han® HPR panel feed-through housing The mating face of the conductor housing is identical to that of the standard surface-mounted housing  This means that you can continue to use your exis- ting hoods  With panel feed-through housings, it is possible to pre-assemb- le the complete connector  They can also be used to create cable-to-cable connections  Outer side Inner side Panel Han® HPR Panel feed-through III-2.12 Han® 48 M Locking lever on housing without ratchet 45 III III – Connectivity products III-2.5 Han® HPR TrainPowerLine Figure III-2.13 Han® HPR TrainPowerLine The Han® HPR TrainPowerLine (TPL) Y-distributor represents an alternative to existing inter- face solutions for train busbars (ZSS) such as those according to UIC 552, although there is no interoperability with existing systems  Cabling with the TPL offers weight advantages of about 14 kg or 22% per wagon com- pared to UIC 552 interfaces and requires less space  The TPL is tailored to modern trains that always run in a network and thus enables a reliable supply of all electrical devices and systems of the train  The solution from HARTING also reduces the complexity of the application: Many variants that were previously required for the construction of busbars can be replaced with this uniform interface  Interfaces in the underfloor and roof area are often hard-wired  With the Han® HPR TrainPowerLine, these can now be plugged in for all connection points in the wagon  For power transmission, high-current contacts from the Han® HC Modular series are used, which transmit up to 800 A and up to 5100 V DC/3000 V AC (to 25 kV surge voltage test)  The die-cast aluminum housings are based on the Han® HPR housings that have been successfully used in the railway sector for decades  These are corrosion-resistant and de- signed for use in harsh outdoor environments  Special features • The connectors of the Han® HPR TrainPowerLine (TPL) are significantly lighter than previous UIC 552 standard solutions  • Uniform parts are used: a Y-distributor and single pole connectors with finger safe protection  • The pluggable solution enables pre-assembly instead of the previous hard- wiring on the coach  • If a coach is uncoupled, the finger-proof connector at the wagon transition can be released  From now, the contact will be sealed and protected with a blind cover against touch and contamination  The blind cover is screwed onto the Han® HPR TPL Y-distributor and can be loosened on site with two screws  46 III III – Connectivity productsCar bodyConnection cableJumper cableFigure addition, cables with a diameter of up to about 13 5 mm can easily be connected to the housing for a 9-pole D-Sub connector (refer to Chapter V, Assembly manuals)  Excellent EMC characteristics are achieved through the labyrinth design in combination with the crimp flange technology with shield- ing attenuation of > 60 dB to 500 MHz  For this purpose, the shielding braid is crimped over the entire surface (360°) using a crimp flange and a sleeve, and then it is connected to the housing with low resistance  55 III III – Connectivity products III-2.9.1 InduCom metal hoods and housing Table are being transmitted  So it is important to implement the proper installation technology to ensure that your electrical equipment achieves high reliability and availability  Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) is an important factor, whereby components should emit the lowest level of interference (emissions) with the high level of interference resistance (immission)  The ideal method is minimising electromagnetic signals at the source  HARTING supports the installation of electrical equipment with enclosures that have very good EMC properties because of their special (e g  labyrinth) construction  Table III-3.1 Han® housing series with excellent EMC characteristics ◦ Han® EMV ◦ Han® 3 A EMV ◦ Han® HPR ◦ Han® 3 A HPR ◦ Han® EMV / B ◦ Han-Modular® Compact ◦ Han-Yellock® 10 ◦ Han-Compact® A continuous, homogeneous transmission of the shielding screen will ensure optimal EMC quality for a connector  or optimum EMC, the connections at the transitions should therefore be as overlapping, large-area, low-impedance and all-round contacting as possible  This is the way HARTING realises its connectors  Figure III-3.1 Key points for EMC shielding Transition from the cable shield to the cable clamp Transition from the bulkhead mounted housing to the electrical cabinet Transition from the cable clamp to the hood Transition from the hood to the bulkhead mounted housing 60 just a few milliamps all the way up to 650 amps  Multi-pole contact inserts such as the Han E®, Han® EE or Han D® series, Han DD®, modules from the Han-Modular® series and high-current contacts from the Han® HC series are all available for these connections  These product series are described on the following pages  Typical contact inserts and contacts for railway applications are listed in tab- les III-4 1 and III-4 2  HARTING’s connectivity products are plug-in connectors  As with all plug-in connectors, you should never pull out the connectors while there is a “live” voltage present! The Han® industrial connectors have been designed in accordance with IEC 61 984  They have the following technical specifications: Insulation resistance: > 1010 Ω Mating cycles: ≥ 500 (with contact resistance ≤ 5 mΩ) The DIN 41 612 / IEC 60 603-2 directive is valid for DIN 41612 connectors  Insulation resistance: > 1012 Ω Mating cycles: ≥ 500 mating cycles; hazardous gas test; IEC 60 603-2 requirements level 1 The InduCom connector and D-Sub are subject to the DIN 41652 / IEC 60807 directive  Insulation resistance ≥ 1010 Ω Mating cycles ≥ 500 mating cycles; hazardous gas test; transmitting data, signal and power between two printed circuit boards  Straight female connectors as well as straight and angled male connectors are available  There are coaxial contacts, signal contacts up to 2 A and power contacts for 6 A, 15 A or 40 A  The connection to the PCB is fixed by means of pre-assembled snap-in clips, screws or guide pins  All modules have THR solder connections and are suita- ble for voltages up to 600 V (CTI value: > 600)  They are tested according to DIN 41612 connector standard IEC 60603-2  Figure III-4.3 Possible configuration of a har-modular® connector (female and male) The modules of a connector are held together by two fixing rails  A total of 31 different connector lengths between 20 32 and 172 72 mm can be realised  The assembly is quick and simple, if quantity exceeds 200 pieces, HARTING offers the delivery of pre-assembled connectors  Finally, users can also use the configurator at www HARTING com to design indivdual har-modular® connectors to suit their specifications  82 III III – Connectivity products15 A power contactsFixing railCoaxial contactsSignal contacts6 A power contacts40 A power contactsGuide pins made of plastics or metalFigure III-4.4 Example of a har-modular® assembly: female connector, angled version 83 III III – Connectivity products III-4.8 M12 Circular connectors Figure III-4.5 Different M12 designs Due to the numerous coding options, an M12 interface can be used for a wide variety of transmissions  Origi- nally used primarily for the transmissi- on of analogous signals, M12 circular connectors today reliably supply appli- cations with data up to 10 Gbps and power up to 7 5 kW  They thus offer a space-saving, robust and reliable solu- tion for railway applications  Sharing the same design, power and high data rates, they can contribute to the ad- vancing digitalisation of trains  New features include K- and S-codings for power transmission  Protected by a full metal housing, the interfaces not only bring the desired performance into the device, but also meet the strict specifications for fire load according to EN 45545-2, as well as for vibration safety and EMC  HARTING M12 circular connectors can be locked and unlocked without tools  By “PushPull” the cable side is simply plugged onto the appropriate device side where it locks securely and with an audible “click”  To release the connection again, the locking ring on the housing is pulled back  The PushPull system saves time and is shock and vibration resistant according to IEC 61373, Cat  1 B  All variants achieve protection class IP65/67  Table III-4.6 Overview of M12 versions Co- ding Po- les Design Gender Cable connection Locking Voltage / power Conductor cross-sectionPower & SignalA 4/5 straight / angled male / connectors for motors and drives used in railway vehicles are a key factor when it comes to saving time and costs during the installation  Depending on the type of railway wagon, the electrical and mechanical pa- rameters are specified by different requirements  The HARTING industrial connectors have a modular design that allows you to make many combinations of housings and contact inserts  They can be used with shielded or unshielded cables  The power connectors are based on housings from the Han® HPR series, size 24 B  These are available as one-part (enclosed housing) or two-part (ope- ned housing with mounting cover) construction  The variant with a two-part housing provides a high degree of convenience and safety, when processing shielded cables  85 III III – Connectivity products 200 A 16 - 35 mm² (PE) 35 - 70 mm²on request 88 III III – Connectivity products III-5.2 Han® HPR EasyCon – high power connector with two-part housing The Han® HPR EasyCon is based on the design of the High Pressure Railway (HPR) housings, and shares the essential technical features of that series  However, in contrast to other HPR products, it uses hood and surface moun- ted housings with mounting covers and contact holding frames  The result is an open system that makes the assembly process much easier for many applications  The connector is available in sizes Han® 16 HPR EasyCon, Han® 24 HPR EasyCon and Han® 34 HPR EasyCon  It is designed specifically so free ac- cess is offered to cable ends, glands and contacts during the assembly  Only at the very end are hoods and housings pushed over the connector and fas- tened  As a result, cables no longer need to be guided through cable glands and housings before being pushed back into the housing after connecting the contacts  Figure III-5.3 Basic construction Surface mounted housing Self- clamping clip Hood Mounting cover External PE terminal Han® HC Modular contacts Mounting plates Cable clamp 89 III III – Connectivity products Figure III-5.4 Mounting cover with cable gland and self-clamping clip The Han® HPR EasyCon housings are usually used for high-current contacts  In addition to suitable hol- ding frames for the Han® HC Modu- lar 250 A, 350 A and 650 A contact inserts, special Han-Modular® hol- ding frames are available for data and signal transmission  Up to six in- dividual modules can be accommo- dated in the Han® 16 HPR EasyCon, up to eight in the Han® 24 HPR Easy- Con and up to twelve in the Han® 34 HPR EasyCon  To achieve an interfe- rence-free and secure transmission, the Han-Modular® mounting frames can be supplemented with shielding and strain relief clamps  All sizes of the Han® HPR EasyCon series are designed for straight cable- to-cable connections  In addition, the Han® 34 HPR EasyCon size is availa- ble as an angled cable-to-cable variant  The Han® 16 and Han® 24 HPR EasyCon hoods can also be combined with the Han® 16 and 24 HPR enlar- ged bulkhead mounted housings  In these cases, the Han® enlarged bulk- head mounted housings are installed on the device side, and the Han® HPR EasyCon hoods on the cable side  A special case is the Han® 24 HPR EasyCon Short  This hood is approx  30 mm shorter than the standard version, but still offers all the advantages of the Han® HPR EasyCon housing series  In addition to the mounting lids with straight cable entry, there are lids with side cable entry  Up to eight individual Han- Modular® modules can be installed using the corresponding holding frames  In combination with the “standard” and “enlarged” Han® 24 HPR bulkhead moun- ted housings, this is an ideal solution for data and signal transmission in the field of car-to-car transitions  Figure III-5.5 Han® 24 HPR EasyCon Short equipped with Han-Modular® inserts, Han® 34 HPR Transformer connector Han® HPR HPTC   Figure III-5.6 Han® HPTC transformer connector, mating and device side, in HPR enclosures The Han® HPR HPTC forms the secondary interface of a trans- former mounted on the rail ve- hicle  With this pluggable inter- face, the connection between transformer and converter can be established quickly and safe- ly  Depending on the require- ments, both shielded and un- shielded connections are possible  The integrated 16-way me- chanical coding prevents from uncorrect insertion  Due to its resistance to UV, ozone and corrosion as well as the testet IP protection class, the Han® HPR HPTC is suitable for indoor and outdoor use  The suitability for indoor and outdoor use as well as for the transmission of high alternating currents was tested in accordance with the standards EN 50467:2012 and EN 60137:2018  Figure III-5.7 Han® HPTC application example 99 III III – Connectivity products Technical specifications Electrical data according to DIN interlocked) IP66 / IP68 Termination type Crimp, cable lug 100 III III – Connectivity products III-5.4 Han® HPR VarioShell housings The Han® HPR VarioShell hood/housing concept is primarily used as a space and material saving jumper cable solution due to its design at a 90 degree angle  However, the VarioShell can also be used flexibly in other positions of rail vehicles to enable space-saving solutions  Power, signal and/or data cables can thus be routed both under the wagon and then along the wagon wall up to the connector and also directly through the wagon wall, with the Han® HPR VarioShell serving as a panel feed- through housing  When the VarioShell is used this way, even two connectors can be placed in the housing, thus saving space and costs  The housing can also accommodate all types of Han® contact inserts, i e  the high-current contacts Han® HC 250 A, 350 A and 650 A, the Han-Modular® inserts with up to two times eight modules or Han® standard inserts  The two-piece design with separate cover results in an open system that both simplifies the pre-assembly and provides quick access for maintenance and repair  In addition, when using a mounting frame, you have the option of me- chanically intercepting the cable or implementing a screen support  Figure III-5.8 Han® HPR VarioShell with Han® HC power contacts (left) next to a panel feed- through version with up to 2 x 8 module slots (middle and right). 101 III III – Connectivity products III-6 Types of connections used in connectors HARTING connectors can be selected with a variety of wire connection types, depending on the following factors: • Wire layout • Tool availability • Installation location • The installation location of the connector The wire connection methods used for railway applications include crimp, cage clamp, screw and axial termination connections  All of these connec- tions offer a high degree of quality and reliability  Additional information can be found in the following sub-chapters, the termination techniques are described in brief  104 III III – Connectivity products III-6.1 Screw terminal The screw terminals are designed according to DIN EN 60999  A distinction is made between terminals with wire protection and terminals that offer no wire protection  The wire protection mechanism consists of a metal tongue that is pressed down by the terminal screw onto the wire (refer to Figure III-6 1)  This prevents the wire strands from shearing off during the installation  The wire ends do not need to be prepared in any special way other than merely stripping them  Figure III-6.1 Figure III-6.2 Screw terminal Screw terminal with wire protection without wire protection There is no metal tongue on the screw terminals that have no wire protection (Figure III-6 2)  So wire ends must be fitted properly with wire-end ferrules  The following table lists the required tightening torque and testing torque for the screw terminals  Table III-6.2 Tightening and test torque for screw terminals Wire cross-section (mm²) 1 5 2 5 4 6 10 16 Screw thread M3 M3 M3 5 M4 M4 M6 Test tightening torque (N m) 0 5 0 5 0 8 1 2 1 2 1 2* Min  pull-out force for stranded wire (N) 40 50 60 80 90 100 * For terminal screws without heads (Han® K 4 / 8) 105 III III – Connectivity products III-6.2 Cage-clamp terminal This termination technique uses the force of a spring to clamp down the wire  The advantage of this type of connection is that there is minimal operational overhead or tool work  It offers a high level of functional reliability since the spring is constantly pressing on the contact  DIN EN 60999 specifies the re- quirements for the construction and inspection of the spring terminals  The advantages: • Use with solid and stranded wires • No need to process the wire ends in any special way • Strong clamping force applied as wire cross-section increases • Wire connection is resistant to vibrations and shock • Cage-clamp terminal has a constant, low voltage drop One wire per termination point          Slot for screwdriver Assembly: • Wire cross-section   - Han®: 0 14 – 2 5 mm²; DIN: 0 14 – 1 5 mm² • Insulation stripping length - Han® ES, Han® Han®:       3 0 x 0 5 mm         – DIN 41612 Bauform H:  2 5 x 0 4 mm 106 III III – Connectivity products III-6.3 Han-Quick Lock® termination technique The Han-Quick  Lock® termination technique was designed to increase effi- ciency during the assembly of plug-in connectors  This termination technique combines the reliability and ease of use of the standard cage clamp termination with the minimal space requirement of the crimp connection  It is the only connection method that can be installed in the field that features a contact density comparable to a crimp connection  Features: • Quick, simple and robust termination technique • Can be installed on-site without any special tools • Resistant to shock and vibration just like the standard cage clamp termination • Mating compatible with the other proven Han® contact inserts Figure III-6 3 shows the layout of a contact with the Han-Quick  Lock® ter- mination technique  Note the spring which applies radial clamping pressure around the wire strands  This special connection method is characterised by a reduced contact resistance  SpringContact with cone Slide Figure III-6.3 Schematic of the Han-Quick Lock® termination technique Crimp termination Crimp wire connections are classified as “solder-free electrical connections”  The specifications for this connection method are defined in DIN EN 60352-2  The goal is to create solder-free electrical connections using hand crimp tools or crimping machines which fulfil the specified mechanical, electrical and climatic requirements  The key to this process is making sure that the tool, crimping sleeve and wire are all well matched to each other  The advantages of the crimp termination technique are: • Can be processed with crimping machines or with manual crimping tools • Connections are produced efficiently • Crimp quality is consistently high when the tools are used properly • No change in resistance as result of heat • Wire under the crimp connection remains flexible The test of a good crimp connection is the wire pull-out force  This force is spe- cified in DIN EN 60352-2 for stranded wires with cross-sections up to 10 mm²  These specified pull-out forces for the crimp contacts are maintained when HARTING crimping tools are used in a proper fashion  The wire pull-out forces are listed in the following table  VDE 0220 is valid for crimp connections of conductors with wire cross-sec- tions above 10 mm²  Table III-6.4 Pull-out forces for crimp connections in accordance with DIN IEC 60 352-2, A2 Cross-section / wire gauge Pull-out force  6 00 10 360 Han® C 10 00  8 380 Han® C 109 III III – Connectivity products III-6.4.1 Crimp connections for wire cross-sections of 10 – 240 mm² Crimp contacts are available for wire cross-sections from 10 to 240 mm²  They can be processed using the crimping jaws according to DIN EN 46235  The crimpings can thus be processed with pressing tools (e g  from the com- pany Klauke)  The crimp contacts are suitable for fine-standed cables in accordance with DIN EN 60228 / VDE 0295 class 5  Deviating cable structures must be che- cked separately  Refer to Chapter V, Assembly manuals, for multiple strands in a single crimping sleeve  In order to create a reliable connection, be sure to follow these points: • The wire combination should be appropriate, regarding material and surface  • The cross-section in the crimping sleeve must be appropriate in relation to the total cross-section of the wire  • The requirements concerning the tensile strength and contact resistance for the connection, according to EN 60352-2, must be met  • All processing steps should be carried out carefully  • The wire strands may not be twisted  • Be sure to maintain the creepage and clearance distances according to the relevant standards  111 III III – Connectivity products III-6.5 Axial screw termination An axial screw wire termination can be used to connect fine-stranded wires (a separate test is recommended when using super fine-stranded wires)  This connection method was designed for connecting wires with large cross- sections in a tight space without any special tools  Wire cross-sections from 2 5 to 185 mm² can be connected  This connection method is suitable for use in railway wagon applications because it offers excellent reliability and resistance to shock and vibration  Finely stranded wires Insert Axial cone Figure III-6.4 Scheme of the axial screw termination Figure III-6 4 shows a cut-out view of a contact with an axial screw termina- tion  The installation instructions for the corresponding contact inserts are de- scribed in Chapter V, Assembly manuals  112 III III – Connectivity products III-6.6 THT solder termination Conventional soldering technology has been proven over decades and provides maximum mechanical stabi- lity and process safety  The soldering pins of the connectors are inserted into plated-through circuit board holes and can then be automatically and simultaneously wave soldered to other components  III-6.7 Press-in technology This solderless connection technology is based on pressing a pin in a plated- through hole printed circuit board  By using a modern, elastically defor- mable press-in zone, the tolerances of the PCB hole can be compensated for  The high electrical and mechanical requirements, as well as the insertion and the high-holding force can thus be met  The press-in technology is an affordable and unlimited processing method, especially for selective gold plating of pins for backplane bus sys- tems  113 III III – Connectivity products III-6.8 IDC (insulation displacement connection) IDC (insulation displacement connec- tion) can be used to provide an easy and safe contacting of solid and stran- ded wires  In one step, the IDC cut- ting terminal penetrates into the wire insulation and resiliently contacts the wires  The gas-tight connection pro- vides maximum safety for even the smallest currents and voltages  A spe- cial feature of the HARAX® IDC is that the cutting terminal is combined with a wire guide element enabling simp- le on-site assembly without special tools  The technical requirements for IDC terminals are standardised in IEC 60352-3  114 III III – Connectivity products III-7 Tools & accessories III-7.1 Overview of tools and accessories PushPull V4 HARTING PushPull V4 is a comprehensive product portfolio with robust de- vice connection technology of protection classes IP65/IP67, intended for the transmission of optical and electrical data as well as of signals and power  The product line offers a multitude of contacts for small installation spaces, thus enabling high data transmission rates and high signal integrity for device communication  Modules are available for data transmission (RJ45), trans- mission through optical fibre (FO) and for power supply (AC/DC)  Also availa- ble: multi-pole inserts for the transmission of analogous as well as digital sig- nals and other standardised interfaces such as USB 2 0/3 0 and DisplayPort  Intuitive locking mechanism The PushPull locking system is easy to handle and requires only little space  An inner, circular profile guarantees a self-locking, durable and tightly sealed device connectivity during the mating process  The user can intuitively re- lease the locking mechanism correctly in one step, as no additional locking elements are required  The force required to mate the connector is reduced to a minimum  An audible “click” signals a successful plugging  The connec- tion can be installed as easily as disconnected, for example, when service is required  Once the locking ring is released, the connector can be easily remo- ved from the device  A circumferential internal seal ensures that both hoods and housings meet protection class IP65/IP67 in mated condition  PushPull – Features •  Easy to handle, low mating forces • Open to various combinations, or pre-assembled with system cables • High contact density • Hoods/housings made of plastics or metal • Includes a clip preventing unintentional releases of the locking mechanism IP65 / IP67 . . . . . . . . . 277 152 IV IV – Assembly manuals IV-1 General notes This chapter contains instructions for the most commonly found connectors in the railway industry to make the assembly or the installation of connectors on site easier . It is the customer’s responsibility to evaluate the suitability of the com- ponents described here and the applicability of the specified regulati- ons for use in any special applications which have not been anticipated by HARTING. The company reserves the right to make construction changes in res- ponse to manufacturing requirements, improved quality or advances in the design. The information in this user’s guide describes component assemblies. No characteristics are guaranteed. Further information can be found in the “Han® Industrial Connectors”, “De- vice connectivity” and “DIN 41612 Connectors” catalogues . 153 VI IV – Assembly manuals IV-2 Inserts with axial screw termination systems The axial screw connection was designed by HARTING for connecting wires with large cross-sections . In compliance with the HARTING philosophy, no special tools are required for the connection . Finely stranded wires can be safely connected using a conventional hexagon torque wrench . Table IV-2 .2 shows an overview of wire cross-sections, stripping lengths and the required tightening torque for selected Han® inserts with axial screw connection tech- nology . The illustration in Figure IV-2 .1 shows the designations used for the dimensions in the table below . The specified cross-sections of the wires refer to the geometric cross-sections of the cable being used . The specifications refer to the catalogue “Han® Industrial Connectors” . According to DIN EN 60228 / VDE 0295 concerning cables and insulated lines, a cable’s cross-section is determined by the conductivity (Ω/km) and the maximum wire diameter . A minimal wire cross-section is not specified (for example: nominal cross-section – 95 mm² ⇨ real geometrical cross-sec- tion – 89 mm²) . Before starting assembly, users can check the cable they wish to use at our accredited HARTING laboratory in regards to the possibility for connection . Additional information about the axial screw wire termination technique Strain relief: The cable should be mechanically supported at an appropriate distance from the connector in order to avoid damage to the contact (e .g ., damage resulting from twisting or pulling of the cable) . The maximum gap for such a strain-relief support mechanism is specified in the following table . Table IV-2.1 Retention gap for easily accessible cables, DIN VDE 0100-520: 2003-06 Outer diameter of the cable [mm] Maximum gap for the fixing support [mm] horizontal vertical D ≤  9 250 400  9 < D < 15 300 400 15 < D < 20 350 450 20 < D < 40 400 550 154 IV IV – Assembly manuals Maintenance for the terminal connections: In order to avoid any splitting of the wire strands, it is only permitted to reapply torque to the connection one time during the entire application lifespan . Cable: The axial screw connection is suitable for finely stranded wires according to DIN EN 60228 / VDE 0295 Class 5 . Cable constructions that deviate should be checked separately . Insert dimension (ISK) for the cable marking Markings for the correct position on cables with contacts using the axial screw termination technique: The user can attach a marker to the cable sheathing in order to specify the proper point for tightening the axial screw on the connecting cable . If the cable is pushed into the insulating base up to the marker (where the marker is flush with the upper edge of the insulating base), the cable is in the proper position and may be connected . Figure IV-2 .1 illustrates this process when using the Han® HC Modular 350 contact . The marker and the upper edge of the insert are at the same level (as indicated by the dashed line) .    1: Stripping length 2: Insulator dimension (ISK dimension) 3: Max . cable insulation diameter 185 mm²:17 1826 .5828 161 VI IV – Assembly manuals IV-3 General installation information for contacts with axial screw termination Observe the following instructions when connecting cables to contacts with axial screw termination technique: 1 . Strip the wire strands according to the preceding table . (The stripping length depends on the type of contact and insert that are being used .) 2 . Insert the wire strands into the contact chamber until the insu lation is flush with the contact . 3 . Hold the wire strands in position . Use the corresponding hexagonal dri- ver at the plug-in side and tighten with the proper torque . The size of the hexagonal wrench and the torque value depend on the type of contact and contact insert being used . These values can be found in the preceding table (Table IV-2 .2) . Figure IV-3.1 A schematic of the axial screw termination 162 IV IV – Assembly manuals IV-3.1 Han® HC Modular 350 The following assembly manual applies for both the one-pole and multi-pole connectors . Step 1 The diameter of the cable’s outer insulation may not be greater than 19 .5 mm . Strip the cable to a length of 19 mm . Step 2 Guide the cable (A) through the hood (B) . Step 3 Press the Han® HC Contact (C) onto the end of the cable . Hold the contact and cable end firmly in order to prevent twisting movement and torsion . Use a torque wrench to apply the specified torque according to Table IV-2 .2 . All cable strands must be completely inserted into the contact chamber . B A C 163 VI IV – Assembly manuals Step 4 Put the support frame (D) into the hexagon-shaped HC contact . The required coding for the HC contact can be made in 60° steps . The figure below shows an example of a one-pole variant . These steps are also valid for the multi-pole variants of the connector . Step 5 Push the contact with the support frame into the hood . Step 6 Tighten the four M3 fixing screws and attach the pressure screw on the cable gland according to the manufacturer’s specifications . M 3 Tightening torque: 0 .5 N m Tools 0 .5 x 3 .5 AF 5 (09 99 000 0371) D 164 IV IV – Assembly manuals IV-3.2 Han® HC Modular 650 Notice: The following instructions are valid for both the one-pole and multi- pole connectors . Step 1 The diameter of the cable’s outer insulation may not be greater than 26 .5 mm . Strip the cable to a length of 23 mm . Step 2 Guide the cable (A) through the hood (B) . B A Step 3 Press the Han® HC contact (C) onto the end of the cable . Hold the contact and cable end firmly in order to prevent twisting movement and torsion . Use a torque wrench to apply the specified torque according to Table IV-2 .2 . All cable strands must be completely inserted into the contact chamber . C 165 VI IV – Assembly manuals Step 4 Put the support frame into the H structure of the HC contact . The desired coding system of the HC contact can be adjusted in 90° steps . Step 5 Screw on the support frame on both sides with the hex bolts and using hex- agonal screws (width across flats 2 .5) . Step 6 Insert the contact packet into the hood . Tighten the four M3 fixing screws and attach the pressure screw on the cable gland according to the manufac- turer’s specifications . Check by inspection hole! 179 VI IV – Assembly manuals IV-8 Coding options for Han® HC Modular contacts The high-current contacts in the Han® HC Modular 250, 350 and 650 series can be coded . This permits multiple connectors of the same type to be in- stalled next to each other without any risk that connectors could be plugged into the wrong sockets . The number of the coding positions differs however . Han® HC Modular 250 offers two, Han® HC Modular 350 six, and Han® HC Modular 650 four coding positions . The actual coding options depend on the number of contacts used in one connector . The contacts are fitted with corresponding coding pegs and grooves for this purpose (refer to Figure IV-8 .1) . Coding groove Coding peg Figure IV-8.1 Coding pegs and grooves on the Han® HC Modular contact IV-8.1 Coding positions Han® HC Modular 250 Figure IV-8 .2 shows both possible coding positions . Figure IV-8.2 Coding positions for the Han® HC Modular 250 180 IV IV – Assembly manuals IV-8.2 Coding positions Han® HC Modular 350 and 650 The contacts have a hexagon-shaped profile (the Han® HC Modular 350) or H-shaped profile (the Han® HC Modular 650) so that they can be installed in different positions in the frame . The details of this can be seen in figures IV-8 .3 and IV-8 .4 . Figure IV-8.3 Hexagon-shaped profile on the Han® HC Modular 350 contact The frames are put onto the hexagon or H-shaped profile of the contacts . This allows a variety of different coding positions to be configured . Figure IV-8 .5 illustrates the available coding positions for the Han® HC Modu- lar 350 contact . There are six different positions that can be coded . Figure IV-8.5 Coding positions for the Han® HC 350 Modular contacts Figure IV-8.4 H-shaped profile on the Han® HC Modular 650 contact 181 VI IV – Assembly manuals The Han® HC Modular 650 contacts offer a maximum of four coding positions (refer to Figure IV-8 .6) . Figure IV-8.6 Coding positions for the Han® HC 650 Modular contacts Before beginning the assembly, be sure that both mating sides of the coded positions fit to each other and can be plugged in together . 182 IV IV – Assembly manuals IV-9 Doubled contacting with axial screw contacts It is generally possible to connect two cables with one axial-screw contact . However, in order to avoid any remaining risk we would recommend to con- tact us before carrying out such an installation . Our laboratory can then check your contact-cable configuration for the appropriate stripping lengths, torques, pull-out forces and temperature characteristics . If the characteris- tics are proper, we can authorise the configuration . Subsequent to authori- sation, installation instructions can be customised to your requirements and application . Figure IV-9.1 Figure IV-9.2 Doubled contacting on contact Doubled contacting on contact Han® HC Modular 650 Han® HC Modular 350 of two cables, each 35 mm² of two cables each 25 mm² wire cross-section wire cross-section 183 VI IV – Assembly manuals IV-10 Han® HC Modular 350 and 650 high-current contacts with screw terminal In addition to the high-current contacts which use the axial screw termination technique, it is also possible to connect cable lugs to high-current contacts . The cable shoes can be fitted to cables with 120 mm² wire cross-sections (for the HC 350) or to cables with 70 to 240 mm² wire cross-sections (for the HC 650) . On the connection side, they consist of a flat washer, a retention clip and a hex screw (M10 for the HC 350 and M12 for the HC 650) . During the installation of the cable shoe, be sure that the components that are placed over the hex screw are placed on in the proper order . 1 . Cable shoe 2 . Flat washer 3 . Retention clip You must also apply counter pressure to the counter support (as shown in Figure IV-10 .1) of the contact using a spanner wrench (Han® HC Modu- lar 350: AF 17, Han® HC Modular 650: AF 24) . This will stop the spread of the torque . If you do not take this step, the insert can be damaged and the inter- face may malfunction . The recommended torque is 14 N m for the Han® HC Modular 350 and 16 – 18 N m for the Han® HC Modular 650 . These contacts are intended only for installation in Han® HPR bulkhead mounted housings! Early release mechanism In the event of an operational error, it is possible to release the crimp tool before it has completed the crimp . •  Take the pressure off the ratchet by pressing gently on the tool’s handles . •  Move the release lever – located in the recessed area next to the item number – to the left until the tool releases the crimp contact . The go/no-go gauge (09 99 000 0889) can be used to check the crimp tool . The four-indent crimp tool may only be used when it is a in proper tech- nical condition. It must be operated in a proper and safe manner. The manufacturer is precluded from liability for damages that result from unauthorised alterations or improper use of this tool. There is no need to set the crimping depth with a plug gauge . If necessary, you may use a go/no-go gauge (09 99 000 0889) to check that the crimp tool is functioning properly .       1 . Insert the crimp contact into the tool opening Ⓐ . The locator ensures that the crimp contact is automatically posi- tioned in the proper crimping position . 2 . Insert a properly stripped wire into the crimp contact Ⓑ . 3 . Press the crimp tool’s handles to- gether in order to crimp the contact . Check for the proper stripping length, external damage, cracks, etc .    NOTICE Opening and releasing in the event of an opera- tional error Never open or close the four-indent crimp tool with force! Otherwise you endanger the functionality of the crimp tool . Ratchet lock The HARTING Four-indent crimp tool 09 99 000 0888 features a ratchet lock Ⓐ for ensuring that each crimp contact is produced with consistent quality . This ratchet lock prevents the crimp tool from being opened prematurely during the crimping process . This ensures a consistent high-quality crimp! Early Release In the event of an operational error, it is pos- sible to release the crimp tool before it has completed the crimp . Take the pressure off the ratchet by pressing gently on the tool’s handles . Using a screwdriver, turn the unlocking screw Ⓐ counter-clockwise . Operational errors can happen when the con- tact is inserted incorrectly or when the wrong contact type is used . 200 IV IV – Assembly manuals IV-15 Hand crimp tool 09 99 000 0110 Operating instructions The crimp tool 09 99 000 0110 is designed for application of various HARTING contacts, cross-section range 0 .14 – 4 .0 mm² . To process these contacts, the tool is fitted with a fixed, turnable locator which enables simple and secure handling . Ratchet mechanism In order to ensure a constant crimp quality for all contacts, the crimping tool is equipped with a releasable safety catch . • It prevents the crimping tool from closing as long as the crimping jaws are not fully opened . • It prevents the crimping tool from opening as long as the crimping cycle is not completed . The mechanism guarantees that every single crimping pro- cess achieves the required degree of deformation . Early crimp unclamp It is possible to effect an early release of the safety device with a screwdriver if the crimp is faulty . The safety device is released by turning right or left . The load must be taken off the jaws by applying slight pressure to the handle . Damage of the crimping stamp will thus be prevented in case the contact does not lie correctly in the profile . Caution! Do not use force to open and shut the crimp tool . Lubricate all moving parts weekly . Locator The turnable locator must be used exclusively with the specific contact types, the component is labelled for . The locator ensures that the crimp contact is processed in the correct position / crimp zone . Process the Han® contact by turning the locator into the position . Crimping process 1 . Insert the contact into the proper crimp profile for the cross-section range . 2 . Secure the contact between the slightly closed crimping jaws . 3 . Insert the cable, correctly stripped of insulation, into the contact . 4 . Crimp until the tool opens again . 5 . Remove the crimped contact . 201 VI IV – Assembly manuals Table IV-15.1 Tensile strength of crimp connections according to DIN IEC 60352-2, A2 Wire cross-section / Wire gauge Tensile strength Han® contacts mm² AWG N 0 .14 26  18 D 0 .22 24  28 D 0 .25  32 D 0 .32 22  40 D 0 .50 20  60 D E  0 .75  85 D E  0 .82 18  90 D E  1 .00 108 D E  1 .30 16 135 D E  1 .50 150 D E  C 2 .10 14 200 E  C 2 .50 230 E  C 3 .30 12 275 E  C 4 .00 310 E  C Notice: HARTING reserves the right to effect changes in design which may arise from improving the quality of our products, new developments or changes in the manufacturing process . 202 IV IV – Assembly manuals IV-16 Crimping tool 09 99 000 0377 The HARTING crimping tool 09 99 000 0377 is designed to crimp Han® C contacts with cross-section of 4 mm² (AWG 12), 6 mm² (AWG 10) and 10 mm² (AWG 8), male and female . These are the standard contacts (refer also to the DIN 41612 Connec- tors Catalogue) . Application field The crimp tool is designed for crimping of DIN 41612 crimp contacts (09 02 000 8434 / . . .8444, 02 05 000 1512 /  . . .2512) . These are supplied in carrier strips of approximately 500 contacts per roll (2) . The roll holder (1) caters for two wire crimp profiles, thus guaranteeing easy handling with the best crimp result . a) Crimp profile 0.09 to 0.25 mm² b) Crimp profile 0.14 to 0.50 mm² Each crimp profile crimps the contacts onto the conductors of the wire and the insulation in one step . After the crimping process, the contact is automatically cut from the carrier strip . The wire then is easily removed from the tool with a successful crimp in- stalled onto the wire . Indexing unit With aid of the indexing unit the contact strip moves into the correct position and is terminated as follows: 1 . Assemble the contact roll (2) onto the roll holder (1) holding it in place with the knurled nut (3) . The contacts point towards the roll holder! 2 . Close the tool, until the lock releases and it can be opened . 3 . Insert the contact strips (6) in the direction of the arrow, into the Indexing Unit . En- sure the contact and the retaining plate of the Indexing Unit (5) is positioned fully over the contact and wire insulation as shown in the detail below . 4 . Index the contact strip forward by pushing trigger (7) fully towards the crimping head . Adjustment of indexing unit To produce a crimp contact within the crimp / wire profiles as a) and b), it is necessary to adjust the indexing unit as follows: 1 . Undo the knurled nut (9) . 2 . Move the indexing unit to the left or to the right . 3 . Tighten up the knurled nut (9) again! 4 . Check to see if the contact is in the middle of the respective crimp profile . Crimping process 1 . Ensure that the contact is in the middle of the crimp profile after the indexing . 2 . Close the tool to the first detent . 3 . Insert the wire into the contact . 4 . Close the hand tool completely until the safety ratchet allows the tool to be opened . 5 . Take out the crimped contact . 0 .09-0 .25 (a) 0 .14-0 .50 (b) mm² 212 IV IV – Assembly manuals Notice: The empty carrier strip can be easily broken off behind the bending plate (8) . Changing contacts 1 . Release the indexing unit red release lever (10) in direction “Release” and hold it there . 2 . Pull out the contact roll away from the in- dexing unit towards the roll (2) . 3 . Remove the contact roll . Safety ratchet The hand crimp tool can only be used after it has been opened completely . Each crimp process has to be done fully, before the tool can be opened again . This guarantees a good quality crimp each time . Early release It is possible to open the tool during crimping if necessary: 1 . Push the tool handles slightly together . 2 . Release the safety lock (11) on the rear of the tool by turning it anticlockwise . 3 . Open the tool handles . Maintenance Remove any existing material and dirt and clean the crimp zone regularly . Check the tool to ensure that the profile closes correctly, clean moving parts and oil them lightly (machine oil SAE 20) . HARTING reserves the right to modify designs in order to improve quality, keep pace with technical advancement or meet particular requirements in production . 214 IV IV – Assembly manuals Safety ratchet The crimping tool is fitted with a safety ratchet to ensure that all contacts are crimped to a constant quality. • The hand crimp tool can only be used after it has been opened completely. • This prevents the tool being opened prematurely once the crimping process has started. Early release It is possible to perform an early release of the safety ratchet in case of a faulty crimping. The tool must be released by pressing together slightly. Operate the release lever (refer to drawing) in the direction of the tool head. Damage to the crimping die will thereby be prevented when the contact does not lie correctly in the crimp profile. Do not open or close the tool with force! Lubricate moving parts weekly! Locator The locator (1) is mounted at delivery, but can be loosened with a hexagon key and replaced if necessary. This seal is the same size as the bulkhead mounted housing (refer to Figure IV-29 .1) . The flange gasket grips the housing and also prevents the fixing screws from coming out . This helps to speed up the attachment and assembly procedures . The holes for the fixing screws are located in the sealing area . In order to create a reliable seal, be sure to follow these points: Protrusion to fit into the fixing hole on bulkhead mounted housing Fixing hole Figure IV-29.1 Bulkhead mounted housing with flange gasket Notices: • The customer must take appropriate steps (sealing) to prevent moisture penetrating through the fixing screws (M4) into the housing . • If you are assembling on metal plate walls, it is possible to fit a mounting frame on the other side – as an abutment providing a more secure fixation to the assembly wall (mounting frame available for sizes 6 B, 10 B, 16 B and 24 B) . It is fitted with M4 threaded holes for the screws that attach it to the bulkhead mounted housing . 228 IV IV – Assembly manuals IV-30 HPR housings, sizes HPR 6 B – 24 B The bulkhead mounted housings from this series have an O-ring seal on the bulkhead mounted side . When the housing is properly installed, with this seal IP68 or IP69K protection can be achieved . These housings have through holes for the M6 retaining screws located within the sealed area of the hous- ing (refer to Figure IV-30 .1) . This allows you to use through holes or continu- ous threads on the mounting surface . Such holes and threads will not affect the sealing . If you are installing on metal panel walls, then a proper mounting frame should be used (such as with the Han® B standard housings) to ensure the stability of the mount and the proper housing seal . Mounting frames with M6 threads are available for the HPR housings . Just as with the standard hous- ings, the installation is carried out from the rear side on the mounting wall .      Fixing hole      O-ring seal Figure IV-30.1 Han® HPR bulkhead mounted housing with O-ring seal 229 VI IV – Assembly manuals IV-31 HPR housing, size Han® HPR 3 A Two variants of bulkhead mounted housings are available for this size: •  Straight bulkhead mounted housing •  Angled bulkhead mounted housing The straight bulkhead mounted housing of the Han® 3 A series has an O-ring seal on the installation side and the through holes for the M4 fixing screws are found inside the sealed area . This housing is installed in a similar fash- ion as the HPR housings in sizes HPR 6 B – 24 B . A mounting frame for the HPR 3 A housing is not required because of its small dimensions .        Fixing hole     O-ring seal Figure IV-31.1 Han® 3 A HPR A further seal is found in the plugging area . Since the inserts of the plastic in- serts in size 3 A are fitted with their own seal too, due to the fact that they can be used in different ways in housings of the Han® A, Han® M or Han® EMV se- ries, these must be removed before use with Han® HPR housings .      O-ring seal Figure IV-31.2 Han® 3 A with O-ring seal In order to achieve IP68 protection for inserts, you should use the M3 sealed screw instead of the M3 fixing screw that is included in delivery . The sealed screw part of the delivery of the Han® 3 HPR housing, it can also be ordered separately under article number 09 20 000 9918 . If, during the installation, you determine that the upper and lower sections of the plug-in connector do not interlock properly, make sure that the O-ring seal has already been removed from the male insert . 230 IV IV – Assembly manuals Table IV-31.1 Overview of male inserts, size Han® 3 A Insert Part number Han® 3 A 09 20 003 2611 09 20 003 2633 Han® 4 A 09 20 004 2611 09 20 004 2633 Han® 7 D (for plastic housings) 09 21 007 3001 Han® 8 D 09 36 008 3001 09 36 008 2632 Han® Q 2 / 0 09 12 002 2651 (4 .0 to 6 .0 mm²) 09 12 002 2653 (2 .5 to 6 .0 mm²) Han® Q 2 / 0 High Voltage 09 12 002 2652 (4 .0 to 6 .0 mm²) 09 12 002 2654 (2 .5 to 6 .0 mm²) Han ® Q 3 / 0 09 12 003 3051 09 12 003 3151 Han ® Q 4 / 0 (for plastic housings) 09 12 003 3051 09 12 004 3151 Han® Q 5 / 0 09 12 005 3001 09 12 005 2633 Han® Q 7 / 0 09 12 007 3001 Han® Q 12 / 0 09 12 012 3001 The angled bulkhead mounted housing Han® 3 HPR is delivered with a flange gasket . Such connectors require no housing but do need a strain relief mechanism . For these applications, HARTING offers a se- cure connection via the grip panel and screw adapter (refer to Figure IV-33 .1) . Knurled screws are used to interlock with the connector so that the plug can- not be accidentally pulled out . The grip panel is used for the strain relief . Grip panel Screw adapter, upper part Screw adapter, bottom part Figure IV-33 .1 Grip panel and screw adapter Therefore, HARTING offers complete sets for the cable side (upper part) con- sisting of a grip panel and a screw adapter upper part . These are available for sizes 6 B, 10 B, 16 B and 24 B and, depending on the type, are compatible with standard and modular inserts (refer to Table IV-33 .1) . The screw adapter upper parts and certain grip panels are also available individually . For each screw adapter upper part, there is a suitable bottom part . These are available, independently of the upper part set, in two different variants . The contacts are thereby securely placed over each other . When assembling in housings, the distance therefore also occurs automatically . The gap, as measured between the contact support surfaces of the inserts, must be between 19 .5 mm and 21 mm . If you use screw adapters without a grip panel, it is possible to maintain the safe contact dimensions (19 .5 – 21 .0 mm) by attaching galvanised steel spa- cer washers (4 x 8 x 0 .8, according to DIN EN 28 378, previously DIN 1440) . In mated condition, the washers ensure that the proper gap is maintained between the upper and bottom parts (refer to Figure IV-33 .9) . This locking panel can be used with bulkhead mounted housings of sizes 10 B, 16 B and 24 B with two locking levers (cross lever) . Two versions are available: 09 30 000 9986 for the Han-Easy Lock® locking lever 09 30 000 9987 for the metal lever A locking panel for bulkhead mounted housings in 6  B  –  24  B with 1  Han- Easy Lock® locking lever (lengthwise lever) is available on request from HARTING . Figure IV-37.1 Locking panel The locking panel is designed so that once it is installed the interlock lever can- not be opened by hand . The locking panel can only be installed when the con- necter is interlocked . A cord attached to the housing flange is used to ensure that the locking panel stays in place (refer to the following images) . 1 . The panel is inserted from the top down behind the locking lever . 2 . The panel is pushed through until the metal tongue is sitting completely under the grip of the locking lever . 3 . The metal tongue is now pressing under the grip and thus preventing the lever from opening . 4 . In order to release the connection, you must press on the metal tongue with a screwdriver . This allows the locking clamp to glide over the metal tongue and release the connection . 249 VI IV – Assembly manuals IV-38 M12 Crimp Slim Design, A-coded and D-coded Part number: D-coded 21 03 881 1405 A-coded 21 03 821 1505 Wire cross-section / male contact AWG 22 – 18 / 0 .33 – 0 .82 mm² 09 67 000 3576 AWG 24 – 20 / 0 .25 – 0 .52 mm² 09 67 000 8576 AWG 26 – 22 / 0 .13 – 0 .33 mm² 09 67 000 5576 Recommended crimp tool: 09 99 000 0501 Locator: 09 99 000 0531 Wire diameter: 2 .0 – 2 .3 mm Cable diameter: 5 .7 – 8 .8 mm Degree of protection: IP65 / IP67 Rated voltage: 250 V Rated current: 4 A Working temperature: -40 °C . . . +85 °C Temperature during connection: –5 °C . . . +50 °C Mating cycles: ≥ 500 Recommended tightening torque, cable side: 0 .6 N m D-coded: Fast Ethernet / 100 Mb / Cat . 5 Assembly manual 1 . Ethernet network systems V-1 Network systems Ethernet is a global standard which is increasingly being employed to handle communication tasks on train  It provides an excellent solution for applications requiring high-speed data transmission such as video monitoring  Modern rail vehicles offer many state-of-the-art services including information, entertainment and internet access  HARTING is your ideal partner because we can provide an Ethernet-based network consisting of rail- compliant components throughout the entire train  We have designed reliable and complete solutions for wagon couplings and Ethernet switching to supplement the standard M12, ix or RJ45 interfaces  Figure V-1.1  Line topology with HARTING Ethernet switches Figure V-1 1 shows a layout of an Ethernet network consisting of active com- ponents (such as switches) and passive components (such as data cables and connectors)  This also includes the pluggable interfaces for the periphe- ral devices, cameras, end terminals and access points that are systematically developed further  The following pages describe the components required for setting up a network in rail vehicles: i e  active Ethernet components (switches) and plug-in connec- tors for the wagon interior, for critical wagon couplings as well as for pre-as- sembled system cables  HARTING is looking forward to your inquiry, if you have any special requirements or are looking for a switch which is not listed here  275 V V – Ethernet network systems V-2 Ethernet switches in transportation applications Switches are exposed to vibrations, electrical fields and other external influences in locomotives, drive and control carriages  This field of ap- plication demands extraordinary reliability and longevity from all electrical components  The EN 50155 standard stipulates these requirements  V-2.1 Unmanaged switch Ha-VIS eCon The Ha-VIS eCon families of switches meet the requirements of rail systems and are also suitable for use in electric sub-assemblies in buses  Ethernet Switches in the Ha-VIS eCon series can be used for Fast (100 Mbit/s) or Gigabit (1000 Mbit/s) Ethernet and are ready to plug & play  Both line and star topologies as well as combinations of both are supported  Depending on the type, six up to ten Ethernet devices can be connected to the Ethernet Switch via RJ45 (twisted-pair), ix Industrial® or M12 ports  The unmanaged switches work in the Store and Forward Switching Mode and support anuto-crossing, auto-negotiation and auto-polarity  V-2.2 Switches HARTING offers switches with different housings and connection concepts, which are derived from the respective application in transportation technolo- gy  The design according to EN 50155 has been developed especially for ap- plications in rolling stock and meets the requirements for EMC, temperature range and mechanical stability  HARTING also offers switches of various function classes to suit the respec- tive application  276 V V –                                                  302 284 VI VI – Customer specific solutions VI-1 Introduction For special requirements, HARTING develops individual solutions based on its complete product portfolio that are precisely tailored to the applications of its customers  The range is from system cabling to the complete develop- ment of customer-specific components  Thanks to progressive modularisation, modern rail vehicles are to an incre- asing extent manufactured as assemblies at different production sites  Im- mediately after completion, the vehicles must be ready for worldwide use  To meet the increased requirements resulting from these developments, custo- mer-specific solutions for vehicle technology are indispensable  It is often not enough to rely exclusively on standardised products  Rather, there is a demand for “problem solvers”, i e  components or systems that quickly and reliably fulfill the individual tasks  Standard components, modi- fied parts as well as completely new developments are combined in these systems  The HARTING range of products and services is covering the need for cus- tomer-specific solutions extending from simplified handling through needs- based commissioning to the tailor-made development of complex products and systems  In addition to product development, comprehensive enginee- ring tasks are also performed  The respective HARTING department can rely on a high-performance team of engineers, qualified technical draughtsmen as well as on an accredited in-house laboratory that is testing independently  Material procurement also works autonomously, oriented on customer needs  Thus, maximum flexibility is provided to the customer  Fur ther information can be found on HARTING’s website at: https://www harting com/DE/en-gb/solutions/customised-solutions VI-2 Partners in the design of inter-vehicle couplers In HARTING’s competence centre for wagon gangway solutions, customers are supported by a comprehensive project management team that looks after the entire process from customer enquiry to series delivery  The selection and qualification of suitable materials, such as connectors, cables and cable protection systems, as well as the consideration of current railway standards form an integral part of the service offering  The service package is supplemented by the development of customer- specific panel feed-throughs and individual internal cabling  The aim is to of- fer manufacturers in the railway sector comprehensive interface solutions for inter-vehicle transitions  285 VI VI – Customer specific solutions Figure VI-2.1 Test installation for jumper cables With the help of the route profile and specific vehic- le parameters, the load on the inter-car gangway system can be simulated and the performance of the HARTING solution qualified and documen- ted  Customer-specific so- lutions can also be quali- fied mechanically and electrically on the basis of individual or standardised test sequences  For this purpose, HARTING offers all the skills of its accredited Quality and Technology Centre (HQT) to meet the highest quality requirements  The Competence Centre for Railway Ve- hicle Coupling Solutions thus offers HARTING customers complete solutions for all tasks in the field of coupling systems for railway vehicles  VI-3 Wagon-to-wagon cable assemblies Various data, signal and power lines run through a rail vehicle  A connec- tion of the “lifelines” can also be re- alised across the coupling points of the vehicle’s subsystems  The internal MVB rail bus, the low-voltage power supply, the power transmission for the battery or air conditioning and the vi- deo signal transmission are just a few examples of what has to be connected between the vehicle parts  Since it is necessary to disconnect these lines in the event of servicing, only a solution with connectors can be considered  Figure VI-3.1 Han® HPR jumper system with strain relief on the vehicle 286 – Customer specific solutions VI-5 Data transfer in rail vehicles VI-5.1 MVB / WTB-Servicebox Figure VI-5.1 MVB / WTB-Servicebox The “Multifunction Vehicle Bus” (MVB) is often used as Fieldbus for the data transmission within rail- way vehicles  It is often combined with the “Wire Train Bus” (WTB) to enable the data exchange throug- hout the entire train  HARTING has developed the MVB / WTB Service Box as a quick and easy solution for testing these communication networks  It represents a T-tap in the network topology  The corres- ponding assembled cables can also be obtained  Since the mid-1990s, the MVB has been used for the Fieldbus in vehic- les manufactured by ABB, Adtranz, AEG, Bombardier and Siemens  The MVB connects all key control me- chanisms in the vehicle, including drive controllers, inputs/outputs, the driver’s control displays, decentralised door controls, auxiliary inverters, climate control systems, central control devices and the train safety sytem  VI-5.2 Data networks Larger and faster data networks are also an increasingly important topic in railway technology  Passengers would like to use the Internet on the train via WLAN with their smartphones and tablets, or be informed by the passenger information system  Data from camera systems must also be transmitted, distributed and pos- sibly stored  Data transmission via optical fibre (FOC) is therefore becoming more and more important  Apart from the higher bandwidth and therefore higher data transfer rates, other features are becoming increasingly impor- tant  Fibre optic technology not only enables the transmission over the enti- re length of the train (which is not always possible with copper conductors), optical fibres are also immune to external electromagnetic interference and 290 VI VI – Customer specific solutions can therefore be routed side by side with high-voltage components, even in small installation spaces  Apart from the space savings, the glass fibre has another striking argument to offer: compared to conventional copper cables, optical fibre saves weight and requires smaller cross-sections  In addition to many components for a safe and protected transmission, HARTING offers the engineering, design and production of complete internal fibre-optic cable assemblies for railway vehicles as well as the appropriate inter-vehicle couplings for signal & data transmission  Tailored to the installa- tion situation and environmental requirements, HARTING provides solutions from single components to complete cable harnesses, ready for installation  Figure VI-5.2 Jumper cable with fibre-optic conductors Figure VI-5.3 Fibre-optic cabling for train vehicles 291 VI VI – Customer specific solutions VI-6 Junction and distributor boxes
