important for railway applications as the standards which are only applicable in the railway environment  The following are selected standards, regulations and requirements which are relevant in practice and in accordance with which HARTING connectors are developed, designed, manufactured and tested  VII-2 General standards for connectors The design of connectors and their termination techniques is based on international standards  Interface / DIN connector: D-Sub: • DIN 41652 Connectors for plug-in boards • CECC 75301-802 Harmonized system of quality assessment for electronic components • IEC 60807 Rectangular connectors for frequencies below 3 MHz DIN 41612: • IEC 60603-2 Connectors for printed circuits for frequencies below 3 MHz Industrial connectors: • IEC 61984 Connectors – Safety requirements and tests • IEC 60664-1 Insulation coordination for equipment within low-voltage systems – Part 1: Principles, requirements and tests • DIN EN 175301-801 Detailed specification – high-pole rectangular connectors with round replaceable crimp contacts (for contact inserts from the Han® D series (except for Han® 7 D) and standard housings sized 10 A, 16 A, 16 B and 24 B)  • DIN EN 60529 Degrees of protection provided by enclosures (IP code) 299 VII VII – Selected standards relevant to railway applications Termination technique: • DIN IEC 60352-2 Solder-free connections – Part 2: Crimp connections; general requirements, testing methods and usage notes (crimp wire connection) • DIN IEC 60352-3 Solder-free electrical connections – Part 3: Solder-free accessible insulation displacement connections • DIN EN 60999 Connection material (torque, screw connection) • DIN 46235 Cable lugs for compression connections; cover plate type for copper conductors VII-3 Railway-related standards When considering the rail compatibility of components, the technical compliance with the requirements of many different standards is what is meant  In Europe, these are brought together in the overall standard, EN 50155  Even though this is a European standard, this standard is also often used outside Europe in tender processes  With regard to connectors, EN 50467 “Railway applications – Vehicles – Electrical connectors, determination and test methods” is the relevant standard  In relation to termination techniques, EN 50467 encompasses many more variants (IDC technique, cage clamp terminal and press-in technology) than EN 50155, which applies for electronic systems in rail vehicles  While EN 50155 refers to test methods of electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), environmental impacts (cold, humidity and warmth), protective classes, shock and vibration, EN 50467 provides significantly more detailed test requirements for connectors depending on their installation location  Selected excerpts from EN 50467: • Protection against incorrect mating EN 60512-13-5 • Protection against electric shock EN 61984; EN 50153 • Earthing devices • Locking • IP degree of protection EN 60529 • Service life • Shock and vibration EN 61373 • Corrosion protection • Behaviour in a fire 300 VII REACh CONFLICT MINERALS VII – Selected standards relevant to railway applications Selected standards referenced from EN 50155: • Shock and vibration: EN 61373, Category 1 B by default, Category 2 on request • Environmental testing: IEC 60068 • Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC): EN 50121, EN 61000-4-4 VII-4 HARTING Product Compliance Even though the “Restrictions of the Use of Cer- tain Hazardous Substances” (RoHS) do not ap- ply for means of transportation for passengers or goods, all HARTING products meet the full requirements of this directive  All HARTING products meet the requirements of REACH and are SVHC free (Substances of Very High Concern)  The advantage for our custom- ers is that the requirement to provide informa- tion to third parties, which is very time-consum- ing, is not necessary! HARTING is aligned with the use of conflict ma- terials and has been successfully audited by DQS in accordance with section 1052 of the Dodd-Frank Act  The EICC-GeSI format declara- tion, with details of the smelters used, can be provided on request  VII-5 Business-related certificates        • Quality management system in compliance with ISO 9001        • Environmental management in compliance with ISO 14001        • ISO/TS 22163 certified (IRIS) These certifications are updated in rotation  A copy can be issued on request  301 VII VII – Selected standards relevant to railway applications VII-6 Flammability directives There are many reasons why the railway is considered the safest means of transport in the world, one of which is the strict standards for fire protection  To avoid, or at least minimise, harmful effects on people, the following properties of flammable materials must be met in particular  1  The materials must be inflammable so that a fire does not spread easily  2  The smoke produced by a fire must be as non-toxic as possible and transparent to reduce the risk of suffocation and to keep the escape routes visible  3  In special areas, the products must be fire resistant, this is also to prevent a fire spreading in the train  Figure VII-6.1 Fire resistance test of Han® connectors: < 1 min (top) and > 15 min (below) 302 VII VII – Selected standards relevant to railway applications The European flammability standard EN 45545 is also used across Europe as a benchmark for fire protection, making it currently the most important standard in this area  To take the different series and operating classes of rail vehicles into account, EN 45545 defines three hazard levels (HL)  The requirements are lowest at HL1, and highest at HL 3  HARTING complies with the highest level in almost all of its rail products  Fire behaviour: Tests of connectors can be performed according to the requi- rement sets R22, R23, R24 or R26  Requirement set R26 is for small electro- technical components, which are listed in the standard under the term “EL10”  These include electronic connectors (M12, D-Sub, SEK    ) as well as terminal blocks or contactors that must meet the requirements for flammability class “V0” acc  to EN 60695-11-10 or UL 94  According to the other sets of requi- rements mentioned, the oxygen index and, if necessary, the smoke density as well as the smoke toxicity must be proof  Fire resistance: For installations between two fire compartments, a resistance to fire of 10 minutes [E10] in technical rooms with volume ≤ 2 m³, and of 15 mi- nutes [E15] for technical rooms with volume > 2 m³ are required  Han® housings achieve the maximum of 15 minutes, and also offer enormous safety buffers  For flammable materials with masses below 10 g, no evidence must be pro- vided  The so-called grouping rule clusters flammable and non-declarable materials which are located at a vertical distance of 20 mm and 200 mm hori- zontal distance respectively  Due to this rule, the limit value can be increased to 100 g for the internal area and 400 g for the external area of the vehicle  HARTING provides its customers with extensive declarations of conformity with regard to EN 45545-2 / -3  Table VII-6.1 Tests and limit values in accordance with EN 45545-2 Test Para- meter Unit Limit value (R22) Oxygen index OI  % HL1: 28 HL2: 28 HL3: 32 Smoke density Ds max  non-dimen- sional HL1: 600 HL2: 300 HL3: 150 Smoke toxicity CITHLP non-dimen- sional HL1: 1 2 HL2: 0 9 HL3: 0 75 303 VII VII – Selected standards relevant to railway applications Flame spread: If surfaces of non-flammable components (e g  made of metal or glass surfaces) are coated in organic materials (i e  flammable materials), these components must also be tested in compliance with ISO 5658-2 or EN ISO 9239-1  The heat released as well as the smoke generated or toxicity of the smoke must only be certified when the nominal coating thickness is larger than 0 15 mm for components in the internal area or larger than 0 3 mm for components on the outside  The coatings on HARTING connectors also meet these requirements with a large safety factor  VII-6.1 Standards for the North American market Conformity with further specialised standards is often required for the North American rail market  Connectors are often not directly considered in these standards, so there is not compatibility in every point  Analogous to standard EN 45545, there is a combination of material requirements with the indices of smoke density, smoke toxicity, heat release and flame spread  The main standards are: • ASTM E 662 – Smoke density • ASTM E 162 – Flame propagation • ASTM E 1354 – Heat and visible smoke release rates • SMP 800 – Smoke toxicity NFPA 130 brings together the requirements mentioned above  VII-6.2 Fire loads A further safety element in the train is that the vehicle manufacturer must determine and specify the total fire load, that is to say the total weight of all flammable materials  To determine the fire load for projects support, HARTING provides the required information on request  304 VII VII – Selected standards relevant to railway applications
